---
title: "HW04: Programming Skills"
draft: false
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE)
```

# Overview

**Due: ~~Thursday, July 16~~ Friday, July 17 (11:59 PM Chicago Time) **

In this last assignment, you will practice the programming techniques learned in class.


# Accessing and Cloning Your `hw04` Repository

**Accessing the repo:** 

1. Go at [this link](https://classroom.github.com/a/sEo5OPEc) to accept the invitation and create your private `hw04` repository on GitHub. Your repository will be named `hw04-<USERNAME>` and will be ready within a few seconds. 

2. Once the repository has been created, click the link provided to access it.


**Cloning the repo into your Workbench:**

1. Log into *Workbench* and start a new project:  
   *File > New Project > Version Control > Git*

2. In the *Repository URL* field:
   - Paste the URL of your GitHub repository. To find the URL, go to your `hw04` repo on GitHub, click the green Code button, and copy the SSH or HTTPS link (use the method you configured during setup: Workbench users must select SSH).
   - The project name should auto-fill. If not, type `hw04-<USERNAME>` manually.

3. Choose where to save the project directory locally. *Tip:* Avoid moving folders after cloning them.  

4. Check the box *"Open in new session"* (recommended for keeping projects separate and organized)

5. Click *Create Project*. This will:
   - Create a new folder on your machine  
   - Initialize a Git repository linked to GitHub  
   - Open an RStudio Project in a new session


# General Homework Workflow

See [Homework 1](hw01.qmd) for details. The workflow is the same for all assignments (e.g., accept the repo, edit the files, save, then Pull – Stage – Commit – Push).


# Assignment Description

In this homework assignment you will practice programming concepts in R that we covered in this module through guided exercises

::: {.callout-note}

- This homework is designed to be completed over multiple sessions—it will feel overwhelming otherwise. Start early, and remember to save, commit, and push your work regularly.

- Do not use AI tools to generate R code from scratch or to think for you; that defeats the purpose of the assignment and violates our class policy on plagiarism. You may use AI to debug (after trying on your own) or look up how functions work (check course materials first). Only submit code you fully understand. Reach out if you have questions, we are here to help!
:::


# Formatting Guide

See [Homework 2](hw02.qmd) for details. The formatting expectations are the same for all assignments.


# Submit the Assignment

To submit your assignment on Canvas, follow these steps:

1. Before the deadline, push the final version of your work to your GitHub repository. *Tip:* don’t wait until the end to make your first commit—commit, stage, and push your work regularly as you go.

   Your GitHub repository should include (make sure to stage-commit-push all these files):
   - `programming.Rmd`: main file you will work on
   - `programming.md`: you will generate this from the `.Rmd` by simply knitting it
   - the folder that contains the graphs that you generated in your `.Rmd`, if any

2. When you're ready to submit:
   - Copy the URL of your GitHub repository. It will look something like: `https://github.com/css-sum26/hw04-yourusername`
   - Submit that URL on Canvas under HW04. Do not upload any files to Canvas. We only need the repository link.


# Assessment

All homework assignments are evaluated using [this rubric](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1gm2bbrG4hriL6Y9XZXZzyn0c_QSCSaLR/edit?usp=sharing&ouid=112534119211880791899&rtpof=true&sd=true). For more, see the *Assessment* section of the Syllabus.
